skill.name: minimax-pdf
skill.description: Use this skill when visual quality and design identity matter for a PDF. CREATE (generate from scratch): "make a PDF", "generate a report", "write a proposal", "create a resume", "beautiful PDF", "professional document", "cover page", "polished PDF", "client-ready document". FILL (complete form fields): "fill in the form", "fill out this PDF", "complete the form fields", "write values into PDF", "what fields does this PDF have". REFORMAT (apply design to an existing doc): "reformat this document", "apply our style", "convert this Markdown/text to PDF", "make this doc look good", "re-style this PDF". This skill uses a token-based design system: color, typography, and spacing are derived from the document type and flow through every page. The output is print-ready. Prefer this skill when appearance matters, not just when any PDF output is needed.
skill.summary: Generate polished, print-ready PDFs with a token-based design system for reports, resumes, proposals, and forms.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported a risk score of 100/100 with 696 findings across 12 files. After manual evaluation, the vast majority are false positives. All HIGH-severity findings (weak cryptographic algorithm, C2 keywords, Windows SAM database) are false positives: hex color codes were misidentified as weak crypto, benign design terminology triggered C2 keyword detection, and the Windows SAM finding is a false pattern match. Medium-severity findings flagged external_commands in markdown files (design/design.md, README.md, SKILL.md) which contain only documentation and code examples, not executable code. The actual scripts (make.sh, cover.py, render_cover.js) execute expected commands for PDF generation: running Python and Node.js scripts, installing dependencies via pip and npm, and using Playwright for headless browser rendering. Network access is limited to loading Google Fonts for cover rendering. Filesystem operations are limited to creating temporary files and output PDFs. No malicious intent, data exfiltration, or credential theft was detected.

medium_findings.title: External command execution in shell scripts
medium_findings.description: The make.sh script invokes python3, node, npm, and npx to execute PDF generation pipelines. Commands are hardcoded and arguments are passed through CLI parsing, not user-controlled input. This is legitimate for a PDF generation tool but represents an elevated privilege boundary.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Commands are hardcoded with user arguments passed through controlled CLI flags, not raw shell injection. Legitimate use case confirmed.

medium_findings.title: Headless browser execution via Playwright
medium_findings.description: render_cover.js launches a Chromium browser via Playwright to render HTML covers to PDF. The browser loads local HTML files and Google Fonts. This is standard for HTML-to-PDF conversion but expands the attack surface.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Playwright loads local HTML files only (file:// protocol). Google Fonts are loaded via CSS @import, not user-controlled URLs.

medium_findings.title: Auto-install of packages with --break-system-packages flag
medium_findings.description: make.sh cmd_fix uses pip install --break-system-packages to force-install Python packages. This flag bypasses PEP 668 protections and could overwrite system-managed packages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The --break-system-packages flag is explicitly used, which is a known risk for system stability. Only runs when user invokes 'make.sh fix'.

low_findings.title: Google Fonts network requests at render time
low_findings.description: Cover HTML references Google Fonts via @import URLs. Each PDF generation triggers network requests to fonts.googleapis.com. Fonts are not cached locally.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed: _gfonts_import() constructs @import url() for Google Fonts. URLs come from predefined palette data, not user input.

low_findings.title: Temporary file creation in /tmp
low_findings.description: make.sh uses mktemp -d to create working directories for intermediate files (tokens.json, cover.html, cover.pdf, body.pdf). Files are cleaned up after completion.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: mktemp -d is a secure way to create temp directories. Cleanup with rm -rf happens after each command completes.

low_findings.title: Environment variable usage for inter-process communication
low_findings.description: make.sh passes data between processes via environment variables (PDF_ABSTRACT, PDF_COVER_IMAGE, PDF_TOKENS). These contain user-provided content, not secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Environment variables carry document content between shell and Python. No secrets or credentials are involved.

content.user_title: Generate professional PDFs with design systems
content.value_statement: Creating polished, print-ready PDFs often requires design expertise and expensive software. This skill applies a token-based design system to generate reports, resumes, proposals, and filled forms directly from content, with consistent visual identity across every page.
actual_capabilities: Generate styled PDFs from structured content (JSON) with 15 document type presets including report, proposal, resume, portfolio, academic, and more
Fill interactive form fields in existing PDF files by inspecting field names and writing values
Reformat existing Markdown, text, or PDF documents into professionally styled PDFs
Render visual elements including charts (bar, line, pie), flowcharts, data tables, code blocks, and mathematical formulas
Apply consistent design tokens (color palette, typography, spacing) across cover and body pages
Auto-install required dependencies (Python packages, Playwright, Chromium) with a single command
limitations: Cover fonts load from Google Fonts at render time and require an active internet connection
Charts, flowcharts, and mathematical formulas require the optional matplotlib Python package
Body pages use ReportLab system fonts (Times, Helvetica) and do not support custom font embedding
PDF form filling supports text, checkbox, dropdown, and radio fields but cannot modify layout or add new fields

use_cases.title: Business report generation
use_cases.description: Create quarterly strategy reviews, financial reports, or client proposals with professional formatting and consistent branding. Users provide content as JSON blocks and the skill handles all visual design decisions.

use_cases.title: Resume and portfolio creation
use_cases.description: Generate visually distinctive resumes and portfolio documents from structured content. Choose from document types optimized for professional presentation with appropriate typography and layout.

use_cases.title: PDF form processing
use_cases.description: Inspect existing PDF forms to discover available fields, then programmatically fill them with structured data. Useful for batch processing application forms, surveys, or registration documents.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic report PDF
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PDF report titled "Q4 Marketing Review" with the type "report". Include sections for executive summary, key metrics, and recommendations.
prompt_templates.scenario: A team member needs a quick, professional report without design work.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a styled resume
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a resume PDF for a senior software engineer named "Alex Chen" using the resume document type. Include sections for experience, skills, education, and a brief professional summary.
prompt_templates.scenario: A job seeker wants a polished resume without using design software.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a PDF form
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the form fields in application.pdf, then fill them with: name "Maria Garcia", email "maria@example.com", position "Data Analyst", and start date "March 1, 2026".
prompt_templates.scenario: An administrator needs to complete a standard application form.

prompt_templates.title: Reformat an existing document with custom styling
prompt_templates.prompt: Reformat the document in quarterly-notes.md into a professional PDF using the "report" type. Title it "Quarterly Team Notes", set the author as "Engineering Lead", and use an accent color appropriate for a technology company.
prompt_templates.scenario: A manager has plain text meeting notes and wants them transformed into a presentable document.

output_examples.input: Create a proposal PDF titled "Website Redesign Proposal" for a creative agency client
output: A multi-page PDF with a split-panel cover design (dark left panel with geometric right side), Syne display font for headings, and Nunito Sans for body text. The document contains formatted sections with accent-colored rules, callout boxes for key insights, data tables for pricing, and consistent typography throughout.

output_examples.input: Fill the form fields in onboarding.pdf with new employee details
output: A completed PDF form with all text fields populated, checkboxes marked, and dropdown selections applied. The original layout, design, and formatting remain unchanged. Output saved as onboarding-filled.pdf.

best_practices: Run `bash scripts/make.sh check` before first use to verify all dependencies are installed, and `bash scripts/make.sh fix` to auto-install missing packages.
Choose document types that match your content purpose: use report for data-heavy documents, resume for personal profiles, and proposal for client-facing materials.
Always run `fill_inspect.py` before filling a PDF form to discover the exact field names and accepted value formats.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for simple text-to-PDF conversion when visual design is not important. Use a simpler tool for basic PDF generation.
Do not attempt to fill PDF form fields without running the inspect step first, as field names must match exactly.
Do not expect cover fonts to work offline. The cover HTML loads fonts from Google Fonts at render time and requires internet access.

faq.question: What document types are available for PDF creation?
faq.answer: The skill supports 15 document types: report, proposal, resume, portfolio, academic, general, minimal, stripe, diagonal, frame, editorial, magazine, darkroom, terminal, and poster. Each type has a unique color palette, cover pattern, and typography pair.

faq.question: Do I need to install anything before using this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill requires Python 3.9+, Node.js 18+, the reportlab and pypdf Python packages, and Playwright with Chromium. Run `bash scripts/make.sh check` to verify dependencies and `bash scripts/make.sh fix` to install them automatically.

faq.question: Can this skill modify existing PDF layouts?
faq.answer: No. The FILL route only populates existing interactive form fields. It cannot change layout, add elements, or modify the visual design of an existing PDF. For restyling, use the REFORMAT route which parses content and creates a new document.

faq.question: Does this skill work without an internet connection?
faq.answer: Partially. Body pages render offline using system fonts. However, cover pages load fonts from Google Fonts and require internet access. Charts and math rendering (via matplotlib) work offline.

faq.question: What content block types can I include in a PDF?
faq.answer: Supported blocks include: headings (h1, h2, h3), body paragraphs, bullet and numbered lists, callouts, tables, images, figures, code blocks, mathematical formulas, charts (bar, line, pie), flowcharts, bibliographies, dividers, captions, page breaks, and spacers.

faq.question: How do I customize the accent color for a document?
faq.answer: Use the `--accent` flag with a hex color value (for example, `--accent "#2D5F8A"`). The skill also auto-derives a lighter accent_lt variant. You can also override the cover background color with `--cover-bg`.
